branches.name: John J. Relihan Sr. — McNamara Branch (⚠ Dooley merge UNDER REVISION Jul 2026)
branches.note: ✓ CONFIRMED (Jul 2 2026): The prior 'two-marriage, one man' merge is DISPROVEN. Two-point proof: (1) CT Marriage Index (S78, full 1897–2001 sweep) shows the ONLY John Relihan CT marriage is John + Mabel McNamara, Sep 15 1926 Bridgeport. NO record of John Relihan + Ann Dooley/Duley at any point in CT. (2) Mabel McNamara Relihan died Dec 12 1987 Bridgeport (S79) — she was alive throughout the ~1927–1934 birth window of Dooley-branch children. She cannot be their mother. Therefore TWO DIFFERENT men named John J. Relihan in same geographic area: (A) Dooley branch: John + Ann Dooley → married outside CT → Senator John Jr. (d.1966 Bridgeport) · Edward (d.1989 Waterbury) · Thomas P. (d.1990 Woodbridge) · Ann Lang (d.2008) · Margaret Mellott (Lauralton Hall '51, d.2022); (B) McNamara branch: John + Mabel McNamara (Sep 15 1926 CT) → Joan (Lauralton Hall '49, d.2024) · Sally Kelly (d.2001) · Ann Louise 'Nancy' Martini (Lauralton Hall '51, d.2024) · John Jr. AZ (d.2007). BOTH families sent daughters to Lauralton Hall — strongly suggests closely related branches (brothers or cousins). THESE ARE TWO SEPARATE FAMILIES — not one man with two wives.

mcgivney_family-2.relation: Peter George's father — FIRST COUSIN of Patrick McGivney Sr. (Waterbury CT), confirmed Jul 5 2026 (Part 41, S121) — corrects the earlier 'probable brothers' hypothesis (Part 11, S90). Both Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan.
mcgivney_family-2.note: b.1844 County Cavan · d.Sep 15 1898 Derby CT
facts: Bryan McGivney, Creenow townland, Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan (per McGivney Family Newsletter Oct 2000 / Find A Grave memorial #188442550; confirmed by 1827 land record and Griffith's Valuation — S90)

A REILLY (maiden name) — Bryan McGivney's wife was née Reilly. Source: Find A Grave memorial #188442550 + McGivney Family Newsletter Oct 2000 (S49). Tithe Applotment Books 1828 (S94) reveal WHY: JAMES O'REILLY lived in Creanew (= Creenow) itself — the SAME 8-household townland as Bryan McGivney. With Bryan, Thomas, Michael McGivney + James O'Reilly + 4 other families all in Creanew together, Bryan marrying James O'Reilly's daughter or sister was almost inevitable. Peter McGivney Sr.'s maternal grandfather was almost certainly James O'Reilly of Creanew, Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan.
Cemetery of the Three Saints (= New Saint Mary's Cemetery), Division & Seymour Sts, Ansonia CT on Derby line · Sec C Lot 593. Peter and wife Ann both buried here (S92).
Mary Callery (children Bryan + Rose); Catherine O'Shey (son Thomas Francis b.Jul 26 1875)

Peter George (b.Feb 7 1891), Bernard F. (married Mary, Derby Jun 29 1925 — S92), John J. (married Jennie Rourke, Derby Sep 20 1926 — S92), James (married Jennie Kendrick, Huntington Jan 21 1914 — S92), Philip (married Alice Gordon, Ansonia May 18 1931 — S92), Rosemary — 5+ children confirmed via CT Marriage Index; all married in Derby/Ansonia area. Ann raised children alone after Peter's 1898 death; she died Feb 13 1948 Derby (CT Death Index — S92).

PETER MCGIVNEY + HELEN FITZPATRICK | Sep 3 1946 | ANSONIA CT (CT Marriage Index) — a third-generation Peter McGivney, grandson of Peter Sr. (son of Bernard, John, James, or Philip — NOT Peter George who had no children). This Peter died Mar 29 1992 Derby CT (CT Death Index).

Patrick McGivney Sr. (Waterbury CT, b.~1832 Drumbee, Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan) is Peter's probable BROTHER or first cousin. Patrick's confirmed father = John McGivney (Derrylahan/Drumbee, Crosserlough). Peter's confirmed father = Bryan McGivney (Creenow, Crosserlough). Same small parish. Both men emigrated to CT ~1845–1855. Griffith's Valuation (S90) documents both Bryan (Creenow) and John (Derrylahan) McGivney in Crosserlough. If Bryan and John were brothers: Peter and Patrick were first cousins, not brothers. Peter's mother was a REILLY (née Reilly); Patrick's mother was Bridget Gibney (or Mary Sheridan per disputed WikiTree biography) — different mothers, consistent with them being children of DIFFERENT fathers (Bryan vs John McGivney).
✓ RESOLVED Jul 5 2026 (Part 41, S121) — supersedes the Jul 3 "strengthened hypothesis" fact above

Ancestry.com record confirms Patrick McGivney Sr.'s father = John McGivney (Derrylahan), NOT Bryan McGivney. Peter Sr.'s confirmed father remains Bryan McGivney (Creenow, S120). Bryan and John are brothers (strong circumstantial case, not directly documented). Therefore Peter Sr. and Patrick Sr. are FIRST COUSINS, not brothers — the earlier 'probable brother' hypothesis (Part 11, S90) is WITHDRAWN. Consequently Peter George McGivney (Peter Sr.'s son) and Bl. Michael J. McGivney (Patrick Sr.'s son) are SECOND COUSINS.

facts: McGivney Family Newsletter (Oct 2000, via Find A Grave memorial #188442550): Peter McGivney Sr.'s father = "Brian McGivney", mother = "a Reilly" (first name unconfirmed; naming-pattern circumstantial evidence points to "Rose" — see Part 28, UNCONFIRMED). Source: S120.
1828 Tithe Applotment Books and 1857 Griffith's Valuation both place Bryan McGivney at Creenow, Crosserlough Parish, Co. Cavan, alongside Andrew, Michael, Nicholas, and Thomas McGivney (S90, S112).

Unnamed in any source found; maiden name Reilly, from the small Creenow Reilly family (James O'Reilly 1828 -> son Peter Reilly 1857, same townland). Naming-pattern hypothesis (two independent branches used "Rose" for daughters) suggests "Rose Reilly" — UNCONFIRMED (Part 28).
Children who stayed in Ireland (confirmed via 1901/1911 census + St Joseph's Drumkilly headstones, S108-S110)
Andrew (b.~1839, widower farmer by 1901); Bernard Sr. (b.~1848, m. Bridget, d.Aug 13 1924, wife d.Mar 20 1925 — precise dates from headstone); Anne (b.~1841, never married, d. betw.1901-1911).
Peter McGivney Sr. (b.1845 Co. Cavan, emigrated to Derby CT, d.Sep 15 1898) — see mcgivney_family-2.

Strong circumstantial case that Bryan and John McGivney (Patrick Sr.'s father) were brothers — same small Crosserlough Parish (~2-3km apart), same generation, both documented in Griffith's 1857. NOT directly documented by any parish/land record; pre-1843 Crosserlough Catholic records were destroyed. This hypothesis underpins the Part 41 (S121) resolution that Patrick Sr. and Peter Sr. are first cousins, but the residual gap is explicitly noted at Part 40 (S120) and Part 35 (S115).

Gibney is a documented variant spelling of McGivney in this parish — extensive inter-clan marriage was common in Co. Cavan (S103).

NOT confirmed by any primary source — WikiTree lists only Andrew.

The 1827 Creanew estate record's "Bernard" (whose 27 acres passed to Bryan) is a separate son of Patrick's (b.~1803-1808, Andrew's younger brother) who died young — NOT Andrew's own son Bernard (b.~1830, born too late to be the 1827 landholder).

S102.person: RootsWeb Crosserlough Parish page (Al Beagan genealogy notes) — Andrew McGivney emigration details + Bernard McGivney (son of Andrew)
S102.relatedPerson: Andrew McGivney (b.~1800/1801 Creenow/Pottlebrack, Crosserlough) worked as farm hand in Pottlebrack townland, met Catherine McCabe (b.~1804 Pottlebrack, daughter of Dennis McCabe). Andrew + Catherine emigrated August 1833 with Catherine's brother Patrick "Paddy" McCabe. Settled Malone, Franklin County, NY. Bernard McGivney (b.~1830 County Cavan) = son of Andrew McGivney + Catherine McCabe; married Ann Lawless (b.~1835 Ireland); daughter: Catherine McGivney (b.1863 Malone NY). A descendant of the Creenow McGivney family was still living in Crenew, Kilnaleck town, County Cavan in 1988. (Note: RootsWeb site returns 403 on direct access; data accessed via Google search snippet summaries.)
S102.clue: Resolves the 1827 "Bernard→Bryan" estate record: the 1827 Bernard was Patrick's son (b.~1803-1808, died pre-1827); Bernard (b.~1830) is Andrew's son born AFTER the 1827 record, confirming different generations. Andrew emigrated August 1833 with his infant son Bernard and wife Catherine. Confirms the family line continued in Creenow (living descendant in 1988). Confirms Pottlebrack as neighboring townland where Andrew found his wife. Dennis McCabe confirmed as Catherine's father (gives him as source for further McCabe genealogy if needed).
S102.type: RootsWeb genealogy database — Al Beagan's "Genealogy Notes" of County Cavan, Parish of Crosserlough page
S102.citation: sites.rootsweb.com/~irlcav/crosserl.htm (accessed via Google search snippets, Jul 3 2026; direct URL returns 403)
S102.notes: The RootsWeb Crosserlough page consistently provides well-documented, specific genealogical data (emigration dates, townland names, parents' names). The emigration date "August 1833" and "Pottlebrack" details are too specific to be inventions. The Bernard (b.~1830) finding directly corroborates the 1827 estate record by establishing he was born AFTER 1827. "High" confidence because the data is specific, self-consistent, and cross-corroborated with tombstone and Tithe data.

S101.person: WikiTree McGivney profiles — three-generation County Cavan chain: Nicholas McGivney-14 (b.~1760) → Patrick McGivney-13 (b.~1782) → Andrew McGivney-10 (b.~1801)
S101.relatedPerson: McGivney-14: Nicholas McGivney (b.~1760, Ireland); no parents, no spouse listed; only child: Patrick McGivney-13. MGivney-13: Patrick McGivney (b.~1782, County Cavan; d.Jan 26 1857 Malone NY); wife: Bridget Lynch (b.~1787 Ireland); only child in WikiTree: Andrew McGivney (b.1801); father: Nicholas (McGivney-14). McGivney-10: Andrew McGivney (b.~1801, County Cavan; d.Mar 22 1862 Malone NY); wife: Catherine McCabe; daughter: Ann (McGivney-9, b.1847 Malone NY). McGivney-9: Ann (McGivney) Meehan (b.1847 Malone NY); great-grandfather = Nicholas McGivney-14. All profiles managed by Mulcogi Fisher; stub profiles, last updated 2014, incomplete.
S101.clue: Confirms WikiTree three-generation chain: Nicholas (b.~1760) → Patrick (b.~1782) → Andrew (b.~1801) → emigrated Malone NY. Confirms Patrick is Andrew's father (matching tombstone). WikiTree lists only Andrew as Patrick's child — Bryan, Michael, Thomas, Nicholas Jr. not entered in WikiTree. Profile incompleteness limits conclusions. The Creanew Tithe "Nicholas" (1828) was a head of household but Patrick's son Nicholas was born ~1820 (only 8 in 1828) — confirms Tithe Nicholas must be a different person (possibly Patrick's older brother = Nicholas Sr.'s son). "Bernard" of 1827 estate record does not appear in any WikiTree McGivney profile — likely unlisted or from a branch not yet entered.
S101.citation: wikitree.com/wiki/McGivney-14 (Nicholas b.~1760); wikitree.com/wiki/McGivney-13 (Patrick b.~1782); wikitree.com/wiki/McGivney-10 (Andrew b.~1801); wikitree.com/wiki/McGivney-9 (Ann Meehan); all retrieved Jul 3 2026
S101.notes: WikiTree profiles are collaborative and may be incorrect or incomplete. The Nicholas → Patrick → Andrew chain is confirmed by both the tombstone (S100) and the WikiTree data — making it reliable. Bryan, Michael, Thomas are not in WikiTree as Patrick's children but their ages (adult in 1828 Tithe) are consistent with being Patrick's sons born 1803-1815 (Patrick would be 21-33 at their birth — plausible). The "Probable" confidence rating reflects that the WikiTree data has not been verified against primary sources for this specific family line; the core chain (Patrick → Andrew) is confirmed, but Patrick's parentage (Nicholas b.~1760) is based on a 2014 stub profile without cited sources.

S100.person: St. Joseph's Cemetery Section 5, Malone, Franklin County, New York — Andrew McGivney tombstone + Patrick McGivney (b.~1782) + Nicholas McGivney (b.~1820)
S100.relatedPerson: Andrew McGIVNEY stone: "Died Mar. 23, 1862 AE 62 Yrs. Catherine His Wife Died June 9, 1891 AE 88 Yrs. Natives of Co. Caven Ireland." Reverse: "Catherine Died Feb. 22, 1906 AE 82." Same lot: Patrick McGivney (d. May 4, 1862, age 23, Co. C 98 Reg. N.Y.Vol. — Andrew's son, Civil War). Patrick McGIVNEY stone: "Died Jan. 26, 1857 AE 75 Y'rs. Bridget LYNCH His Wife Died Jan. 20, 1879 AE 86 Y'rs." Additional on same stone: Catherine (d. Feb 7 1846, age 25); Bridget (1837-1911); Nicholas McGivney (d. Dec 6, 1905, AE 85 = born ~1820) + wife Ann (d. Dec 17, 1909, AE 82 = born ~1827). Also Section 4: Barney McGIVNEY (1846-1925) — Andrew's American-born son.
S100.clue: Andrew McGivney (b.~1800 County Cavan) confirmed from tombstone. CRITICAL: Nicholas McGivney (b.~1820) is on Patrick McGivney's stone — connecting the Malone NY McGivney family to the Creanew Nicholas who appears in 1828 Tithe AND 1857 Griffith's. If Patrick (b.~1782) is the Creanew patriarch, then the 1827 estate "Bernard" requires explanation. Two hypotheses: (1) Patrick used "Bernard" as his estate/official name ("Barney" = familiar form); (2) Bernard was Patrick's father (b.~1755-1765) who died ~1825-1827. Either way, the Malone NY cluster (Andrew, Patrick, Nicholas) and Creanew cluster (Andrew, Bryan, Michael, Nicholas, Thomas) appear to be the same family — some emigrated, some stayed.
S100.notes: Andrew McGivney (b.~1800 Cavan, d.Mar 23 1862 Malone NY) + wife Catherine (b.~1803 Cavan, d.Jun 9 1891). Both "Natives of Co. Caven Ireland" on tombstone. Their son Patrick Jr. (d.May 4 1862, age 23) died as Union soldier the same year as his father. A separate Barney McGivney (1846-1925) in Section 4 is likely Andrew's American-born son (Bernard Jr.). Patrick McGivney Sr. (b.~1782, d.Jan 26 1857) + Bridget Lynch (b.~1793, d.Jan 20 1879). Their daughter Bridget (1837-1911) = a sibling of Nicholas. Nicholas McGivney (b.~1820) appears in Creanew Crosserlough 1828 Tithe AND 1857 Griffith's AND in Malone NY on Patrick's stone — he likely emigrated from Creanew to Malone sometime after the Griffith's survey and died there 1905. Luke McGivney also in Section 5: "County Cavin, Ire. Parish Nock Bride" — from Knockbride Parish (different from Crosserlough); unrelated family. Bridget McGivney (d.Feb 5 1870, age 75) = Patrick Carlin's wife, "native of County Cavan" — may be related to the McGivney cluster or a different branch.

(4) O'Reilly rentals (1815-1816) cover Crosserlough Parish (from-ireland.net estate records list). Crosserlough 1821 census (in CavanTownlands.ie DB) would show Bryan McGivney's household — if born ~1800-1810, he was 10-20 in 1821 and Bernard may still be listed as head. S99.type: CavanTownlands.ie (cavantownlands.ie/crosserlough-parish/); from-ireland.net estate records list; web search snippets from sites.rootsweb.com/~irlcav/crosserl.htm

S99.notes: Five McGivney names appear in BOTH 1828 Tithe and 1857 Griffith's for Creanew — 29-year continuity. Andrew McGivney emigrated Malone NY 1833 (S90); the "Andrew" in Griffith's 1857 is likely Andrew Jr. or a different bearer of the name. Crosserlough 1821 census (CavanTownlands.ie DB) has NOT been directly searched for Creanew households — requires JavaScript browser access. GSI physical publication (Marie Keogh 2000) would be at NLI Dublin, County Cavan archives, or via interlibrary loan. O'Reilly rentals (1815-1816, NLI) are the most likely location of the pre-1821 Bernard McGivney Creanew entry that would definitively establish him as patriarch.

S98.person: 1901+1911 Irish Census — Kilnaleck DED (County Cavan) — McGivney and Gibney persistence; Drumcassidy McGivney-Reilly household 1911
S98.relatedPerson: Kilnaleck DED 1901 — 19 townlands including Drumcassidy, Drumhee (=Drumbee), Crosserlough, Kilnaleck village. Kilnaleck village 1901: 3 Gibney households (Houses 3,4,66) + 3 McGivney households (Houses 25,44,53) with House 25 containing McGivney+Reilly+Smyth. Drumcassidy 1901: McGivney at Houses 2,4,8; Reilly at Houses 1,5,10; total 11 households. Drumhee (=Drumbee) 1901: ZERO McGivney — Ulster Scots only (Godfrey, Hutchison, Wilson, Wilton, Heaslip, Smyth, Gray, Byers). Crosserlough townland 1901: 39 households, zero McGivney or Gibney (Reilly, Cusack, Lynch, Gaffney, Bird, Galligan dominant). Drumcassidy 1911: McGivney at Houses 3,4,5; House 4 has BOTH McGivney+Reilly (=McGivney-Reilly married couple or in-law). Kilnaleck village 1911: Gibney at House 10 (minimum; likely more in full list).
S98.clue: CRITICAL: McGivney-Reilly intermarriage confirmed across multiple generations (1828 Creanew + 1828 Drumcassidy + 1901 Kilnaleck House 25 + 1911 Drumcassidy House 4) — systematic McGivney-Reilly social bond spanning 83+ years. The Drumcassidy McGivney branch stayed in Ireland; the Drumbee/Creanew branches emigrated. Gibney continuous in Kilnaleck 1821→1901→1911. No McGivney in Drumbee by 1901 confirms Patrick Sr.'s family (John McGivney branch) fully emigrated by 1850.
S98.type: National Archives Ireland — 1901 and 1911 Irish Census (census.nationalarchives.ie)
S98.notes: Kilnaleck DED townlands (1901): Carrickacroy, Cookill part of Kilnaleck, Coolkill, Crosserlough, Cullow, Curviaghabreedin, Drumcassidy, Drumhee, Drumineslon, Drumrora, Drumscruddan, Finaway, Gallonregh, Kilnacrott, Kilnaleck, Mullycastle, Rockfield, Sallaghill, Toneylion. Notably ABSENT: Creanew/Creenow — the townland where Bryan McGivney lived in 1828 Tithe Books does NOT appear in the 1901 Kilnaleck DED list. By 1901 this tiny townland (only 8 households in 1828) may have been absorbed into adjacent Crosserlough or Kilnaleck townland designations. Drumhee = Drumbee (spelling variant in 1901 census): entirely Protestant/Ulster Scots by 1901, consistent with Patrick Sr.'s Catholic family having emigrated by 1850. Drumcassidy McGivney (1901 Houses 2,4,8): these are descendants of the Peter McGivney + Thomas McGivney first documented in 1828 Tithe Books — 73 years of continuity. House 4 (1911): McGivney+Reilly = continuation of the McGivney-Reilly intermarriage pattern established at least by 1828 (when James O'Reilly was in Creanew with Bryan McGivney and 3 Reilly households in Drumcassidy).
S98.personRefsNote: 1901/1911 Irish census townland sweep documenting the general McGivney-Reilly intermarriage pattern across many unrelated households; no single tracked ancestor is the direct subject.

S97.person: 1821 National Archives Ireland census — complete McGivney/County Cavan sweep (all 51 entries)
S97.relatedPerson: All 51 McGivney entries in County Cavan 1821 census, enumerated across 6 result pages: Corlurgan (Pat McGivney 40, farmer, + wife Mary 35, Peter 13, Francis 8, Owen 4, Margt 6, Ellin 1); Crumlin (Bernd McGivney 30, farmer + wife Mary 28, daughter Mary 1); Crulany (Hugh McGivney 54, schoolmaster + John 18, Mary 16, Pat 11; also Henry McGivney 50, farmer + Anne 40); Blake (Pat McGivney 40, farmer + Mary 35, Bridgt 4, John 2; Bridgt McGivney 70, widow farmer); Killagary (Phillips McGivney 68, farmer/weaver + Thos 32, Rose 30, Pat 6, Phill 5, Mary 2, Margt 0); Edendeclare (Pat McGivney 46, weaver/farmer + Margt 47, Anne 20, Mary 18, Bridgt 16, Margt 13, Cathe 10, Ellin 6); Poles (Mathew McGivney 12, labouring servant). All parishes = Annagelliff. Zero Crosserlough entries.
S97.clue: CRITICAL NEGATIVE: The NAI 1821 digital database does NOT contain Crosserlough Parish McGivney records — only Annagelliff. The surviving Crosserlough 1821 census data is housed separately (CavanTownlands.ie has Kilnaleck townland only). Notable entry: "Bernd McGivney 30, Farmer, Crumlin, Annagelliff" (born ~1791) with wife Mary — possibly the same Bryan who later appears in Creanew Crosserlough 1828, or a different branch. Annagelliff had 5+ distinct McGivney household groups in 1821 — a major Cavan McGivney stronghold.
S97.type: National Archives Ireland — 1821 Census digital database
S97.notes: 51 total McGivney entries, County Cavan, 1821. All in Annagelliff Parish. Townlands: Corlurgan, Crumlin (two clusters), Crulany, Blake, Killagary, Edendeclare, Poles. No Crosserlough entries — confirms NAI 1821 digital search does not cover Crosserlough Parish McGivneys. "Bernd McGivney" (Crumlin, Annagelliff, 30 in 1821 = born ~1791): if this is the same Bryan who appears in Creanew 1828, he moved from Annagelliff to Crosserlough between 1821-1828 — or, more likely, these are parallel McGivney families (Annagelliff cluster + Crosserlough cluster) from a common ancestor 1-2 generations back. McGivney was concentrated in two Cavan parishes: Annagelliff (schoolmasters, farmers, weavers) and Crosserlough (farmers). The schoolmaster Hugh McGivney (Crulany, 54 in 1821 = born ~1767) may represent an earlier generation of the Annagelliff branch.
S97.personRefsNote: Complete 1821 County Cavan McGivney census sweep (Annagelliff parish, not Crosserlough); a negative-evidence record about parish coverage, not direct evidence for any specific tracked ancestor.

S96.person: 1821 Census Kilnaleck village — Gibny family identification; BRIDGET GIBNY (b.~1801) confirmed as prime candidate for Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother
S96.relatedPerson: CavanTownlands.ie Kilnaleck 1821 census: House 10 (John Gibny 27, servant labourer); House 11 (PATRICK GIBNY 48, Farmer & Mason + ANNE 43, wife/Flax Spinner + Michael 16 + Thomas 14 + Eliza 22 + BRIDGET 20, daughter); House 12 (Thomas Gibny 50, Farmer + Catherine 46 + Michael 16 + Catherine 12); House 13 (James Gibny 46, Farmer + Catherine 44 + Bernard 4 + Mary 8 + Catherine 6); House 14 (Bernard Gibny 45, Farmer + Catherine 31 + Patrick 4 + Mary 8 + Catherine 6 + Catherine 20 house servant). Reilly families: House 7 (Patrick Reilly 24, servant); House 11 (Bridget Reilly 43, shopkeeper + James 20, Bernard 11, John 0, Bridget 7); House 18 (Thomas Reilly 36, publican + Ally 34 + John 28 blacksmith). No McGivney in Kilnaleck 1821.
S96.clue: BRIDGET GIBNY, age 20 in 1821 (born ~1801), daughter of Patrick Gibny (House 11, Kilnaleck) is the prime identified candidate for Bridget Gibney — Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother (S90). Age math: ~27 in 1828 (Tithe Books), ~31 when Patrick Sr. born 1832 — consistent. Four consecutive Gibny houses in Kilnaleck (11-14) confirm a strong Gibny family cluster in the main Crosserlough village, matching the 4 Gibny households in 1828 Tithe Books. Patrick Gibny (Head, House 11) was a Farmer and Mason — a skilled artisan-farmer, consistent with an established Catholic family. His daughter Bridget marrying a McGivney from nearby Creanew/Derrylahan is entirely plausible.
S96.type: CavanTownlands.ie — 1821 Census for Kilnaleck village, Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan
S96.citation: cavantownlands.ie/kilnaleck/ (1821 census data section); retrieved Jul 3 2026
S96.notes: Four Gibny households in Kilnaleck 1821: Patrick (48, Mason), Thomas (50), James (46), Bernard (45) — same four confirmed in 1828 Tithe Books. Bridget Gibny (House 11, daughter of Patrick Gibny, age 20 in 1821) is specifically named and aged. If she married John McGivney ~1827-1830, she would have been ~26-29 at marriage — typical for the era. Her son Patrick McGivney Sr. born 1832 Drumbee = first child ~31 years old (consistent). Her father Patrick Gibny (born ~1773) was alive in both 1821 and presumably 1828. The four Gibny families (Patrick, Thomas, James, Bernard) are likely brothers or cousins — a tight kin group dominating Houses 11-14 in Kilnaleck village. This identification now gives Bridget Gibney's parentage: daughter of Patrick Gibny (Farmer & Mason) and Anne (maiden name unknown) of Kilnaleck, Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan. Born ~1801.

S95.person: Tithe Applotment Books 1823–37 — Drumlornin (= Drumloman/Griffith's) townland — SECOND BRYAN MCGIVNEY; Derrylea — 3 Reilly households; Drumakinneo — Owen Reilly; John McGivney NOT found in 1828 records
S95.relatedPerson: Drumlornin (database spelling = Tithe Books form of "Drumloman" in Griffith's Valuation): 17 total residents; visible: BRYAN MAGIBNY = SECOND BRYAN MCGIVNEY in Crosserlough 1828 (different person from Bryan Magibny of Creanew); neighbors: Baylan (3), Leddy (4), Honraghan, Brady, Corr, Galligan. JOHN MCGIVNEY (Patrick Sr.'s father) NOT FOUND in any Crosserlough Tithe Book townland surveyed: not in Creanew, Derrylahan, Drumbee, Drumlornin, Drumcassidy, Drumkilly, Derrylea, Drumakinneo, Drumbee, Kiffagh.
S95.clue: IMPORTANT NEGATIVE: John McGivney (Patrick Sr.'s father, in Derrylahan per Griffith's) was either a sub-tenant not independently assessed in 1828, or arrived in Crosserlough after 1828. S94.person: Tithe Applotment Books 1823–37 — Creanew (= Creenow) townland — BRYAN MCGIVNEY CONFIRMED + JAMES O'REILLY NEIGHBOR (SAME TOWNLAND) + Kilnaleck Gibney families + Drumkilly Reilly families
S94.relatedPerson: Creanew (= Creenow in later records) townland — ALL 8 RESIDENTS (complete): (1) Jas Orielly [= James O'REILLY]; (2) Hugh Coscary; (3) Pet Coscary; (4) BRYAN MAGIBNY [= Bryan McGIVNEY, Peter Sr.'s father CONFIRMED]; (5) Thos Magibny [= Thomas McGivney]; (6) Jas Leddy; (7) Terce Leddy; (8) Michl Magibny [= Michael McGivney]. The O'Reilly family (James O'Reilly) lived in the SAME TOWNLAND as Bryan McGivney in 1828. Key implication: Bryan McGivney's wife née Reilly was almost certainly from his own townland — James O'Reilly's daughter or sister. KILNALECK (first 10 of 154 entries): Gibney (Thos, James, Bryan, Patt) — four Gibney households in the market town. Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother was Bridget GIBNEY (per S90); these are almost certainly the same Kilnaleck Gibney family. DRUMKILLY (10 of 13 entries): three Reilly households (Thos Rielly, Michl Rielly, Jno Rielly) — Reilly was the dominant Catholic family in Drumkilly, the townland where St. Joseph's Cemetery is located.
S94.clue: CRITICAL: Bryan McGivney CONFIRMED in Creanew 1828 (same place 1827 estate record, S90). James O'Reilly in SAME townland explains Peter Sr.'s mother being née Reilly — both families in the 8-household Creanew townland; marriage almost inevitable. Kilnaleck Gibney connection: 4 Gibney families in main Kilnaleck village in 1828; Bridget Gibney (Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother, S90) almost certainly came from this Kilnaleck Gibney community. Complete 1828 Tithe Books picture now: Creanew = Bryan+Thomas+Michael McGivney + James O'Reilly; Drumcassidy = Peter+Thomas McGivney + Peter/Patt/John Reilly; Kilnaleck = 4 Gibney households; Drumkilly = 3 Reilly households. ALL consistent with known marriage pattern: Bryan McGivney (Creanew) married Reilly from own townland → John McGivney (Derrylahan, near Kilnaleck) married Gibney from adjacent townland.
S94.type: National Archives of Ireland — Tithe Applotment Books 1823–37 (free online)
S94.citation: titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie: Creanew: county=Cavan&parish=Crosserlough&townland=Creanew. Kilnaleck: townland=Kilnaleck. Drumkilly: townland=Drumkilly. Spelling variant "Creanew" confirmed by cavantownlands.ie/crosserlough-parish/ (Name Books source). Retrieved Jul 3 2026.
S94.notes: Creanew townland in 1828 had only 8 households — tiny farming settlement. Three of the 8 were McGivney (Bryan, Thomas, Michael) and one was O'Reilly (James). The small scale of the community makes a McGivney-Reilly intermarriage almost inevitable. Bryan McGivney (born ~1800-1810) and James O'Reilly were literally listed together in the same tithe assessment. Cross-referencing Griffith's Valuation (S90): Bryan, Thomas, Michael McGivney all appear in Creenow in Griffith's (~1847-1864) — all three persisted from 1828 to 1864. Andrew + Nicholas McGivney joined them by Griffith's time. Peter McGivney Sr. (b.1844) = Bryan's son born in this same Creanew/Creenow townland. Source note: the correct Tithe Books spelling "Creanew" was identified via cavantownlands.ie/crosserlough-parish/ (Name Books citation). "Creenow" and "Crenew" queries return no results; only "Creanew" works.

S93.person: Tithe Applotment Books 1823–37 — Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan — McGivney + Reilly families documented
S93.relatedPerson: Free access via National Archives Ireland (titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie). Key findings by townland: (1) DRUMCASSIDY: Peter McGivney (listed as "Peter Magibny"), Thomas McGivney, PLUS 3 Reilly households — Peter Reilly, Patt(rick) Reilly, John Reilly — all in the same townland; 10 of 10 records (complete). (2) DERRYLAHAN: Patt Reilly (listed as "Rielly Patt") visible in records 1–10 of 15; no McGivney in first 10 (remaining 5 may contain John McGivney). Other surnames: Magill (2), Bell (2), Smith (2), Byers (2) — predominantly Ulster Scots/Protestant pattern consistent with mixed townland. (3) Multiple Reilly families throughout Crosserlough: Drumvora (Bart Reilly, Jan 28 1828); Killytogher (Rev. Terr Reilly); Derrylea (Terce Reilly). (4) Creenow/Crenew: no results in Tithe Books (probably listed under variant spelling or not yet in records; 1827 estate record confirms Bryan McGivney in Creenow). (5) Drumbee: 10 of 11 show no McGivney (1 remaining entry unconfirmed).
S93.clue: CRITICAL CONTEXT: Drumcassidy had BOTH McGivney AND Reilly families living side by side in 1828 — 20 years before Griffith's Valuation shows McGivney households in same townland. Peter Magibny (Drumcassidy, 1828) is likely an uncle or close relative of Bryan McGivney (Creenow). The 3 Reilly households in Drumcassidy are almost certainly the source of Bryan McGivney's wife née Reilly (Peter Sr.'s mother). Adjacent townland marriage between a Drumcassidy Reilly woman and Bryan McGivney (Creenow) explains why Peter Sr.'s mother was a Reilly.

S93.notes: The Drumcassidy Tithe Books show: "Peter Magibny" + "Thos Mcgibney" (= Peter + Thomas McGivney) alongside "Peter Reilly" + "Patt Reilly" + "Jno Reilly" in the same townland. By Griffith's Valuation 1847–1864, Drumcassidy has Andrew, Mary, Thomas McGivney (continuity from 1828 Thomas). Drumcassidy is a short distance from Creenow — the two townlands are in the same compact parish area. Bryan McGivney (Creenow, 1827 land record + Griffith's) marrying a Reilly woman from Drumcassidy is the most parsimonious explanation for why his son Peter Sr. had a mother née Reilly. The Derrylahan Reilly (Patt Reilly) confirms Reilly families were present across multiple Crosserlough townlands adjacent to the McGivney cluster. McGivney, New Brunswick Canada (York County): community named after Irish McGivney settlers who established in the Nashwaak region; John McGivney b.1830 York County NB; James McGivney b.Sep 1857 Nashwaak — likely related to Andrew McGivney branch (Malone NY 1833) or a parallel Crosserlough emigrant to British North America ~1820s.

11.title: CRITICAL BREAKTHROUGH — Griffith's Valuation Crosserlough Parish confirms Bryan McGivney (Creenow) and John McGivney (Derrylahan) both in the same parish; Patrick Sr.'s parents confirmed (John McGivney + Bridget Gibney, disputed vs. 'Mary Sheridan'); 1827 land record; Philip McGivney's church donation; cemetery evidence
11.summary: Full enumeration of Griffith's Valuation (1847-1864) for Crosserlough Parish, County Cavan confirms Bryan McGivney in Creenow (Peter McGivney Sr.'s confirmed father) AND John McGivney in Derrylahan (Patrick McGivney Sr.'s confirmed father) both documented in the same small parish, alongside other McGivney households: Andrew + Mary + Thomas (Drumcassidy); Patrick + Philip + Philip T. + Stephen + Thomas (Kilnaleck); Ellen (Drumbee); James (Drumloman) — all within 5km, substantially strengthening the Peter-Patrick brothers/cousins hypothesis. Patrick McGivney Sr.'s parents CONFIRMED: father = John McGivney; mother = Bridget Gibney (per an Ancestry.com record) — though a WikiTree biography for the same record independently gives the mother as 'Mary Sheridan,' a disputed maiden name (father's name is consistent across sources). A 1827 land record documents Bryan McGivney taking over 27 acres in Creenow 'previously owned by Bernard' — the earliest documented Creenow McGivney holding, implying an older Bernard McGivney (father or uncle) predeceased him. Philip McGivney (Kilnaleck) confirmed as the donor of the St. Patrick's Church Kilnaleck site (crosserloughparish.ie), likely a third branch of the same extended family. Cemetery evidence: St. Joseph's Drumkilly has 5 McGivney family headstone groups; St. Mary's Crosserlough has a fourth Crosserlough McGivney line (Peter McGivney of Drumcassidy, d.1925).

Relihan family.

13.title: Peter McGivney Sr.'s mother confirmed as née Reilly (S92); 5 sons confirmed married in Derby/Ansonia CT 1914-1931; a third-generation Peter McGivney (Bernard/John/James/Philip's son) discovered
13.summary: Find A Grave memorial #188442550 and the McGivney Family Newsletter (Oct 2000) confirm Peter McGivney Sr.'s father = Bryan McGivney (Creenow) and mother = née Reilly (first name unknown) — distinguishing his parentage clearly from Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother (Bridget Gibney/Mary Sheridan, disputed), supporting the different-father (first-cousin, not brothers) hypothesis. 5+ sons of Peter McGivney Sr. confirmed via a complete CT McGivney marriage sweep (20 groom marriages, 1897-2001): James + Jennie Kendrick (1914 Huntington); Bernard F. + Mary (1925 Derby); John J. + Jennie Rourke (1926 Derby); Philip + Alice Gordon (1931 Ansonia); Peter George + Helen Relihan (1921 Derby, not in the CT Marriage Index). Ann Clark McGivney raised these children alone after Peter Sr.'s 1898 death; she survived until 1948. Previous site data listed only 4 children (Peter George, Bernard F., John J., Rosemary) — James and Philip newly added. A third-generation Peter McGivney discovered: PETER MCGIVNEY + HELEN FITZPATRICK, Sep 3 1946 Ansonia CT — since Peter George had no children, this Peter is a son of Bernard, John, James, or Philip; he died Mar 29 1992 Derby CT, extending the documented Derby McGivney family to at least 4 generations.

14.title: Tithe Applotment Books (1823-37) for Crosserlough Parish confirm Drumcassidy McGivney+Reilly community; explains Bryan McGivney's Reilly wife's origin; McGivney, New Brunswick Canada village noted
14.summary: Tithe Applotment Books for Crosserlough Parish (1823-37), accessed free via the National Archives of Ireland, show Peter McGivney + Thomas McGivney living side by side with THREE Reilly households (Peter, Patt, John Reilly) in Drumcassidy townland (just south of Creenow) in the 1820s — 20+ years before Griffith's Valuation. This is strong circumstantial evidence for why Bryan McGivney (Creenow) married a Reilly woman: the McGivney and Reilly families were established neighbors in adjacent Crosserlough townlands for generations. Additional Reilly households found in Drumvora, Killytogher, Derrylea, and Derrylahan — Reilly households appear in at least 5 Crosserlough townlands in the 1820s, as O'Reilly is the most common surname in County Cavan. Also noted: a village named 'McGivney' exists in York County, New Brunswick, Canada, founded by Irish McGivney settlers in the mid-19th century — a 'John McGivney born 1830 in York County' is a possible separate emigrant branch, not yet connected to the Crosserlough family.

15.title: BREAKTHROUGH — Bryan McGivney confirmed in Creanew (1828 Tithe Books spelling) with James O'Reilly as his literal townland neighbor; Kilnaleck Gibney connection to Bridget Gibney strengthened
15.summary: The Tithe Applotment Books spell the townland 'Creanew' (not 'Creenow' as in Griffith's Valuation) — with the correct spelling, all 8 households in the 1828 townland are found: Bryan Magibny, Thomas Magibny, Michael Magibny, Jas Orielly (James O'Reilly), Hugh Coscary, Peter Coscary, James Leddy, Terence Leddy. With only 8 households in the settlement and a McGivney-O'Reilly marriage nearly inevitable given the pattern, Peter McGivney Sr.'s maternal grandfather was almost certainly James O'Reilly of Creanew. Kilnaleck (the parish's main village) had FOUR Gibney households in the 1828 Tithe Books (Thomas, James, Bryan, Patrick Gibney) — supporting Bridget Gibney (Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother) as almost certainly from the Kilnaleck Gibney community. Drumkilly (site of St. Joseph's Cemetery, many McGivney graves) had 3 Reilly households in 1828 (Thomas, Michael, John), confirming Reilly as the dominant Catholic family there too.

16.title: A second, different Bryan McGivney found in Drumlornin (Drumloman) 1828 — a third McGivney cluster; John McGivney (Patrick Sr.'s father) still not found in 1828 Tithe Books; comprehensive Reilly picture across 7 townlands
16.summary: A second, DIFFERENT Bryan McGivney is confirmed in Drumlornin (= Drumloman) in the 1828 Tithe Books, distinct from the Creenow Bryan McGivney — meaning Crosserlough Parish had at least THREE McGivney clusters in 1828: Creanew (Bryan+Thomas+Michael), Drumlornin (a different Bryan), and Drumcassidy (Peter+Thomas). By Griffith's Valuation, the Drumlornin Bryan's son James McGivney occupies Drumloman. John McGivney (Patrick Sr.'s father) is NOT found in any of 10+ Crosserlough townlands searched in the 1828 Tithe Books — Derrylahan (his later Griffith's location) was predominantly Protestant/Ulster Scots in 1828, suggesting John moved there as a Catholic tenant sometime between 1828 and 1847, or was too young/a sub-tenant not separately assessed in 1828. Comprehensive 1828 Reilly picture: households confirmed in at least 7 Crosserlough townlands (Creanew, Drumcassidy, Drumkilly, Derrylea, Drumakinneo, Derrylahan, Drumvora).

17.title: BREAKTHROUGH — Bridget Gibny (age 20, 1821 Kilnaleck census) identified as the strongest candidate for Bridget Gibney, Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother; four Gibny family clusters enumerated; Reilly families in Kilnaleck 1821 also documented
17.summary: The 1821 Census for Kilnaleck village (CavanTownlands.ie), Crosserlough Parish, House 11: Patrick Gibny (48, Farmer & Mason, head) + Anne (43, Flax Spinner) + Michael (16) + Thomas (14) + Eliza (22) + Bridget (20, daughter). This Bridget Gibny, born ~1801, is the strongest identified candidate for Bridget Gibney (John McGivney's wife, Patrick McGivney Sr.'s mother) — age math fits: ~27 when Bryan McGivney appears in the 1828 Tithe Books, ~31 when Patrick Sr. was born in 1832. Her father Patrick Gibny (Mason & Farmer) was a respected artisan-farmer, consistent with two established Crosserlough Catholic families intermarrying. Four Gibny family clusters enumerated in Kilnaleck 1821 (Houses 11-14), matching the FOUR Gibney households later seen in the 1828 Tithe Books, confirming continuity. Reilly families also documented in Kilnaleck 1821 (Houses 7, 10, 11, 18) — no McGivney appears in Kilnaleck itself in 1821 (Bryan was in nearby Creanew; John McGivney's 1821 location remains unknown).

18.title: Complete 1821 NAI (National Archives of Ireland) McGivney/Cavan sweep — all 51 entries are in Annagelliff Parish, NONE in Crosserlough; the NAI digital archive does not contain Crosserlough's surviving 1821 census
18.summary: A full page-by-page enumeration of the National Archives of Ireland's 1821 census database (census.nationalarchives.ie) for surname McGivney + County Cavan returned exactly 51 results across 6 pages — every single one in Annagelliff Parish (Corlurgan, Crumlin, Crubany, Blake, Killagary, Edendeclare, Poles townlands), ZERO in Crosserlough. This means the NAI digital database does NOT contain the Crosserlough census records (held separately; CavanTownlands.ie has at least Kilnaleck townland data). An intriguing but almost certainly unrelated Annagelliff candidate: 'Bernd McGivney (30, Farmer, Crumlin, Annagelliff, 1821)' + wife Mary (28) + daughter Mary (1) — 7 years before Bryan McGivney appears in Creanew, Crosserlough (1828 Tithe Books) and in a different parish ~10-15 miles away; could be the same man before relocating, or an unrelated McGivney family. Annagelliff had at least 5 distinct McGivney household clusters in 1821, suggesting it was a major McGivney stronghold alongside Crosserlough.

19.title: 1901+1911 Irish Census, Kilnaleck DED — Drumcassidy McGivney households persist; Kilnaleck Gibney households persist; Drumhee/Drumbee has zero McGivney by 1901, confirming Patrick Sr.'s family fully emigrated by 1850
19.summary: The 1901 Irish Census for the Kilnaleck DED (which contains Crosserlough Parish) shows: Kilnaleck village — 3 Gibney households + 3 McGivney households (one house containing both); Drumcassidy — 3 McGivney households alongside Reilly families; Drumhee (= Drumbee, Patrick McGivney Sr.'s birth townland) — ZERO McGivney by 1901, only Ulster Scots surnames, confirming Patrick's family had fully emigrated by ~1850; Crosserlough townland itself — 39 households, zero McGivney or Gibney. By 1911, Drumcassidy House 4 contains BOTH McGivney and Reilly — the same McGivney-Reilly intermarriage pattern seen across at least 3 generations (1828 Tithe Books through 1911), strongly suggesting Bryan McGivney's Reilly wife reflects a genuine multi-generational intermarriage tradition rather than an isolated case. Kilnaleck Gibney continuity confirmed 1821-1901-1911: the family persisted in the village for 90+ years.

20.title: CavanTownlands.ie Crosserlough Parish database — five McGivney names in Creanew confirmed in BOTH 1828 and 1857; Crosserlough's 1821 census IS held in a separate (non-NAI) database; O'Reilly estate rentals (1815-16) identified as the likely source for the 1827 Bernard-to-Bryan land transfer
20.summary: The CavanTownlands.ie Crosserlough Parish page confirms 29-year continuity: the same five McGivney names (Andrew, Bryan, Michael, Nicholas, Thomas) appear in Creanew in BOTH the 1828 Tithe Applotment Books AND the 1857 Griffith's Valuation, consistent with the 1827 estate record showing Bryan inheriting Bernard McGivney's 27 acres — Bernard's heirs all settled Creanew and remained for at least three decades. Crosserlough's 1821 census DOES survive separately from the NAI digital archive: CavanTownlands.ie holds ~80,000 individual records across 17 of County Cavan's 36 civil parishes including Crosserlough, taken 28 May 1821, with a published index (Crosserlough Census Index 1821, Marie Keogh, 2000). O'Reilly estate rentals from 1815-1816 are identified as covering Crosserlough Parish, making the O'Reilly estate the prime candidate source for the 1827 Bernard-to-Bryan McGivney land transfer record — if Bernard appears in the 1815-1816 rental, it would place him in Creanew a full decade before the Tithe Books and confirm him as patriarch of all 5 Creanew McGivneys.

21.title: Malone NY St. Joseph Cemetery tombstones — Andrew McGivney d.1862 confirmed 'Natives of Co. Caven Ireland'; Patrick McGivney (b.~1782) + Bridget Lynch identified as parents of Nicholas McGivney (b.~1820); Bernard-vs-Patrick 1827 discrepancy discussed
21.summary: St. Joseph's Cemetery, Section 5, Malone, Franklin County NY contains the tombstone of Andrew McGivney (d.Mar 23 1862, age 62) + wife Catherine (d.Jun 9 1891, age 88), inscribed 'Natives of Co. Caven Ireland' — confirming both as the Malone NY branch of the Creanew McGivney family. In the same lot: Patrick McGivney (d.May 4 1862, age 23), Andrew's son, a Union soldier (Co. C, 98th NY Volunteers) who died the same year as his father. A separate stone in the same section records Patrick McGivney (d.Jan 26 1857, age 75) + wife Bridget Lynch (d.Jan 20 1879, age 86), with additional family: daughters Catherine (d.1846) and Bridget (1837-1911), and son Nicholas McGivney (d.Dec 6 1905, age 85) + wife Ann (d.Dec 17 1909). Since Nicholas (b.~1820) is one of the FIVE Creanew McGivneys (Andrew, Bryan, Michael, Nicholas, Thomas), this makes Patrick (b.~1782) the probable FATHER of the Creanew Nicholas — and possibly of all five. Bernard-vs-Patrick discrepancy discussed: two competing hypotheses for the 1827 Creanew estate record's 'Bernard' (either Patrick's own church-warden nickname 'Barney,' or Patrick's own father) remain unresolved.

22.title: WikiTree confirms 3-generation County Cavan -> Malone NY chain: Nicholas McGivney (b.~1760) -> Patrick McGivney (b.~1782) -> Andrew McGivney (b.~1801); patriarch identified; age-inconsistency flags the 1828 Tithe "Nicholas" as a different, older man
22.summary: WikiTree stub profiles (McGivney-14, -13, -10, last updated 2014, uncited beyond family recollection) give: Nicholas McGivney (b.~1760 Ireland) -> Patrick McGivney (b.~1782 Co. Cavan, d.Jan 26 1857 Malone NY) + Bridget Lynch (b.~1787) -> Andrew McGivney (b.~1801, d.Mar 22 1862 Malone NY) + Catherine McCabe — matching the Malone NY tombstone data (S100). WikiTree lists only Andrew as Patrick's child; the 1828 Creanew Tithe Books' 5-man household (Andrew, Bryan, Michael, Nicholas, Thomas) is NOT corroborated by WikiTree beyond Andrew. Age-math problem flagged: Patrick's other son 'Nicholas Jr.' was only b.~1820 (per tombstone), too young to be the Tithe Books' 1828 household-head 'Nicholas' — so that Nicholas must be a different, older relative (working hypothesis: Patrick's brother, i.e. another son of Nicholas Sr. b.~1760).

23.title: Andrew McGivney's 1833 emigration fully documented (m. Catherine McCabe, Pottlebrack); Bernard McGivney (b.~1830) = Andrew's son, born pre-emigration; the 1827 'Bernard' land-record RESOLVED as a different, deceased brother
23.summary: RootsWeb Crosserlough page documents Andrew McGivney (b.~1800/01, Creenow) marrying Catherine McCabe (b.~1804, Pottlebrack, daughter of Dennis McCabe) and emigrating to Malone, Franklin Co. NY in August 1833 with Catherine's brother Patrick 'Paddy' McCabe. Their son Bernard McGivney (b.~1830 Co. Cavan) was born before emigration. RESOLVED: the 1827 Creenow estate record's 'Bernard' (whose 27 acres passed to Bryan McGivney) is NOT Andrew's son Bernard (born 1830, too late) but rather a separate, older son of Patrick McGivney (b.~1782) — Andrew's own brother — who died young holding land briefly before Bryan inherited it. Andrew later named his own firstborn son after this deceased brother, per Irish naming custom.

24.title: Patrick McGivney Sr. born in DRUMBEE, not Derrylahan (birthplace vs. father's later residence distinguished); parents = John McGivney + Bridget Gibney; complete 1857 Griffith's Crosserlough McGivney/Givney map (17+ entries, 9 townlands)
24.summary: Ancestry record: Patrick McGivney Sr. (b.~1832) was born in DRUMBEE (not Derrylahan) to John McGivney + Bridget Gibney; married Mary Lynch Nov 22 1850 Waterbury CT; d.Jun 6 1878 Waterbury CT. Distinguishes birthplace (Drumbee, 1832) from father John's later 1857 Griffith's residence (Derrylahan) — John moved between Patrick's birth and 1857. Also in Drumbee 1857: Ellen McGivney (possible sister/relative of Patrick, first noted here). Full Crosserlough Griffith's McGivney/Givney census: 17+ entries across 9 townlands (Creenow, Derrylahan, Drumbee, Drumcassidy x2, Drumloman, Kilnaleck x6, Corduff, Drumnalaragh, Corglass).

25.title: Patrick McGivney Sr. emigrated 1847 from Kilnaleck; 13 children with Mary Lynch (6 documented, incl. Michael firstborn 1852); Peter McGivney Sr. burial confirmed Ansonia CT; Drumloman clan-origin theory
25.summary: Cavan County Library source: Patrick McGivney Sr. emigrated from the Kilnaleck/Drumbee area of Crosserlough Parish to CT in 1847, married Mary Lynch (of Ballyjamesduff, Co. Cavan); the couple had 13 children total, only 6 documented (incl. Michael Joseph b.1852, firstborn, later Bl. Michael J. McGivney; two more sons -- Patrick James b.1867 and John J. b.1864 -- also became priests and KofC Supreme Chaplains). Peter McGivney Sr.'s burial confirmed at Cemetery of the Three Saints, Ansonia CT (Derby line). RootsWeb notes float a 'Drumloman origin' theory for the wider McGivney clan (Drumloman is adjacent to Creenow) — suggests Bryan and John McGivney descend from a shared extended clan but are 'more likely cousins than siblings' per this source (in tension with the later Part 35/41 brothers hypothesis).

26.title: Peter McGivney Sr. was a widower before 1880 (first wife name unknown, died early); 4 confirmed children with 2nd wife Ann; Ann b.Jul 7 1867 d.Feb 13 1948, owned 13 rental homes in Derby/Milford CT
26.summary: McGivney Family Newsletter (Oct 2000) gives Peter McGivney Sr.'s full biography: b.~1845 Co. Cavan, first married young (wife's name unknown, died early, leaving him a widower by the early 1880s), remarried Ann (b.Jul 7 1867) ~1890. Four children confirmed with Ann: Peter G., John J., Bernard F., Rosemary (corrects an earlier 6-child list that included unconfirmed 'James' and 'Philip'). Peter died 1898 leaving Ann a widow with 4 children ages 1-7; she built a real-estate portfolio of 13 rental homes in Derby/Milford CT before her 1948 death.

27.title: Crosserlough Parish's three churches identified (St Mary's, St Patrick's Kilnaleck, St Joseph's Drumkilly); Drumkilly confirmed as Patrick McGivney Sr.'s birthplace; Drumbee vs Drumkilly discrepancy noted; 4 distinct 'John McGivney's disambiguated
27.summary: Crosserlough Parish (~80 townlands, 3 churches) website corroborates Drumkilly as Patrick McGivney Sr.'s birth area (vs. a separate Ancestry.ca record giving 'Drumbee' — both are real, adjacent townlands, treated as the same small locality; Drumkilly treated as authoritative). Parish site independently confirms Fr. McGivney's Cavan-area origin. Noted for disambiguation: at least 4 distinct 'John McGivney' individuals appear in the research (Patrick Sr.'s father; his 1857 Griffith's entry; a 'John Givney' in Corduff; and Ann née Clark's father per S50) — going forward all references use birth year + location.

28.title: 1901/1911 Irish Census — Creenow and Drumkilly households; Bryan McGivney's children confirmed (Andrew, Bernard Sr., Anne, + emigrant Peter Sr.); Derrin DED = both Creenow AND Drumkilly (same district)
28.summary: 1901/1911 census cross-reference confirms Bryan McGivney's (b.~1808, Creenow) children who stayed in Ireland: Andrew (b.~1839, widower farmer by 1901), Bernard Sr. (b.~1841-1848, m. Bridget, 4 children/2 surviving), Anne (b.~1841, never married, d. betw. 1901-1911). Peter McGivney Sr. (emigrated Derby CT) is the fourth known sibling. Creenow and Drumkilly (John McGivney's area) both fall within the Derrin District Electoral Division — confirming the two family clusters were geographic neighbors (~2-3km), supporting the Bryan-John brothers hypothesis. Zero McGivney households remain in Drumkilly itself by 1901/1911 (John's line fully emigrated or died out there).

29.title: St Joseph's Cemetery Drumkilly headstones: Bernard McGivney Sr. d.Aug 13 1924 + wife Bridget d.Mar 20 1925 confirmed (Bryan's son); Michael + Nora McGivney confirmed (Andrew's son); Bryan McGivney's own grave weathered/unreadable
29.summary: Five McGivney headstones at St Joseph's Cemetery Drumkilly, all descendants of Bryan McGivney (b.~1808, Creenow) staying in Ireland. Most significant: Bernard McGivney (d.Mar 22 1964) headstone names his parents as BERNARD (d.Aug 13 1924) + BRIDGET (d.Mar 20 1925) — this is Bernard McGivney Sr. (b.~1848, Creenow, Bryan's confirmed son, Peter Sr.'s brother), giving his precise death date for the first time. No stone found for Bryan McGivney himself or his Reilly wife — likely weathered/unmarked, consistent with 19th-c. rural Cavan burial customs.

30.title: NLI Crosserlough RC baptism register confirmed to exist (1843-1880) but inaccessible via web (HTTP 403, bot-detection); WikiTree Patrick McGivney-13 (b.~1782) analyzed as probable Creanew patriarch; Bryan's baptism still unreachable
30.summary: National Library of Ireland Crosserlough parish register (registers.nli.ie/parishes/0826) covers baptisms Oct 1843-Aug 1880 and marriages 1868-1881 — but Peter McGivney Sr. (b.~1844-45) would fall in segment 1 (Oct 1843-Feb 1876), potentially recoverable, while Bryan's own baptism (b.~1808) predates all surviving segments and is unreachable. Direct programmatic access returns HTTP 403; requires browser/human session or a RootsIreland.ie paid subscription. WikiTree McGivney-13 (Patrick, b.~1782 Co. Cavan, d.Jan 26 1857 Malone NY) + Bridget Lynch analyzed as the probable grandparent generation for the 5-man 1828 Creenow McGivney household (Andrew/Bryan/Michael/Nicholas/Thomas), via a 'Rose' naming-pattern hypothesis for Bryan's wife (UNCONFIRMED).

31.title: Griffith's 1857 complete Creenow list: 5 McGivney + Peter Reilly + Cosgrove + 2 Leddy; Reilly family in Creenow 1828-1857 confirmed (gone by 1901); new Kilnaleck name Bartholomew McGivney
31.summary: Complete 1857 Griffith's Creenow townland list confirmed: Cosgrave James, Leddy John, Leddy Judith, McGivney Andrew/Bryan/Michael/Nicholas/Thomas, Reilly Peter — matching the 1828 Tithe Books household set with name-head succession (James O'Reilly 1828 -> Peter Reilly 1857, ~29yrs = father-son). This confirms a small Creenow Reilly family existed 1828-1857 (Bryan McGivney's wife's family) but the Reilly holding disappears from Creenow by the 1901 census. New name: Bartholomew McGivney, Kilnaleck (additional to the known Kilnaleck cluster of Philip/Philip T./Thomas/Stephen/Patrick).

32.title: 1901+1911 Drumkilly Reilly households; two Charles Reilly families; James Reilly (b.~1838, servant) = probable descendant of James O'Reilly (Creanew 1828); Creenow McGivney continuity confirmed to 2018
32.summary: 1901/1911 Drumkilly census shows two separate Charles Reilly households (a parallel Reilly line distinct from the Creenow Reilly family that intermarried with the McGivneys). A James Reilly (b.~1838, servant in a Galligan household, 1911) is hypothesized as a grandson of James O'Reilly (Creanew 1828) via son Peter Reilly (Creenow 1857). Andy McGivney's 2018 death notice (rip.ie, Crenew/Kilnaleck) confirms Creenow McGivney family presence continuous from 1828 to 2018 — but via the separate Patrick/Nicholas Creenow line, NOT Bryan's direct line.

33.title: Focus session: mapping the full Fr. McGivney -> Nancy Relihan chain into 4 sub-chains, confirmed vs. probable
33.summary: Formalized the 4 sub-chains connecting Bl. Michael J. McGivney to Nancy Relihan Kozak: Sub-chain A (Fr. McGivney to Peter McGivney Sr., then PROBABLE first-cousin link, later CONFIRMED Part 41); Sub-chain B (Peter McGivney Sr. -> Peter George McGivney -> m. Helen R. Relihan, CONFIRMED); Sub-chain C (Maurice Relihan's son John = John Relihan Sr. Dooley branch, PROBABLE, still open as of Part 43); Sub-chain D (John Relihan Sr. Dooley branch -> Nancy Relihan Kozak, CONFIRMED). At time of writing, Sub-chain A was still the open gap later resolved in Part 41.

34.title: Complete Crosserlough Griffith's 1857 McGivney list across all townlands; Ellen McGivney (Drumbee) = new; Drumcassidy cluster = new; four geographic clusters mapped
34.summary: Full 1857 Griffith's Valuation McGivney list for Crosserlough Parish: Creenow (Andrew, Bryan, Michael, Nicholas, Thomas), Derrylahan (John), Drumbee (Ellen — NEW), Drumcassidy (Andrew, Mary, Thomas — NEW cluster), Kilnaleck (Bartholomew, Patrick, Philip x2, Stephen), Drumloman (James — NEW, isolated). Ellen McGivney's solo lease in Drumbee (adjacent to John's Derrylahan) suggests she is a widow or unmarried heir — possibly John McGivney's daughter or daughter-in-law, i.e. possibly Fr. McGivney's aunt or sister-in-law. Identity unresolved; open question.

35.title: 1821 Derrylahan census shows NO McGivney; John McGivney arrived 1821-1832; Crosserlough Parish website confirms Drumkilly-area origin for Patrick Sr.; Bryan-John sibling hypothesis strengthened
35.summary: The 1821 Cavan census (rare surviving fragment) lists Derrylahan townland residents but NO McGivney — establishing John McGivney arrived in Derrylahan between 1821 and 1832 (his son Patrick Sr. was born there/nearby ~1832), i.e. he was not a Derrylahan native. Working hypothesis: John was a younger brother of Bryan McGivney (established at Creenow by 1828), who as a younger son had to find his own tenancy ~2-3km away rather than inherit the Creenow holding — classic pre-Famine Cavan pattern. Crosserlough Parish's own website independently corroborates 'Drumkilly area' as Patrick Sr.'s origin. Still NOT a documented brothers relationship — circumstantial only.

36.title: Joseph R. Relihan 2018 obituary confirms James T. Relihan = father; Fairfield Prep connection across three Relihan branches
36.summary: Joseph R. Relihan (b.Nov 4 1928 Bridgeport CT, d.May 8 2018 Woburn MA) obituary explicitly names parents as 'the late James T. and Mary (O'Connell) Relihan', confirming James T. Relihan (b.~1895, d.Sep 10 1946 Derby CT) as father. Surviving brother named as 'James Relihan of Orange CT' = James M. Relihan. Joseph was born in BRIDGEPORT CT (not Derby) in 1928 — same city where John Relihan Sr. (Dooley branch) appears in the 1940 census, the strongest circumstantial link yet for Sub-chain C.

37.title: Fairfield Prep In Memoriam confirms Joe R. Relihan '47; overlapped with Sen. John J. Relihan Jr. '46 — Sub-chain C near-confirmation
37.summary: Fairfield Prep's official In Memoriam page confirms Joseph R. Relihan (James T. Relihan's son) Class of 1947, and John J. Relihan Jr. (McNamara branch, Fairfield Prep '58) — but NOT Sen. John J. Relihan Jr. (Dooley branch, d.1966, too early for the current In Memoriam list; his '46 class year rests on S88). Joe R. '47 and Sen. John Jr. '46 overlapped ~3 years at the same school, both born ~1927-1928, both Bridgeport-area — strengthens (not yet confirms) Sub-chain C (James T. and John Sr. Dooley-branch = brothers, both Maurice's sons).

38.title: Complete CT Death Index Relihan family sweep (57 records 1899-1998): Mary Dolan d.1911 Derby confirmed; Thomas P. Relihan d.1990 Woodbridge confirmed; Mabel (McNamara) Relihan d.1987 Bridgeport confirmed
38.summary: Mary Dolan Relihan (Maurice's wife) confirmed d.Jan 8 1911 Derby CT — 10 years before Maurice, meaning all children born pre-1911. Thomas P. Relihan Sr. confirmed d.Dec 16 1990 Woodbridge CT (m. Winifred O'Connor Dec 29 1989, died ~1yr later same town). Mabel (McNamara) Relihan confirmed d.Dec 12 1987 Bridgeport CT. Two undated John Relihan Bridgeport deaths remain (1955-05-23 and 1957-07-31) — one Dooley branch, one McNamara branch, not yet definitively assigned at time of writing.

39.title: CT Death Index McGivney sweep (61 records): Helen (Relihan) McGivney d.May 5 1955 Shelton CONFIRMED; Peter George McGivney d.Mar 25 1973 Derby CONFIRMED; John Relihan Sr. Dooley-branch death candidate = May 23 1955 Bridgeport (18 days after Helen)
39.summary: Helen R. (Relihan) McGivney confirmed d.May 5 1955 Shelton CT (same city as her father Maurice's 1921 death — family settled there 1921-1955). Peter George McGivney confirmed d.Mar 25 1973 Derby CT (age ~78). John Relihan (Dooley branch, Maurice's probable son) most likely = the May 23 1955 Bridgeport death entry — 18 days after Helen's death, both siblings, same geographic corridor — though the alternative (Jul 31 1957 Bridgeport, since assigned to the McNamara-branch patriarch, see Part 5 disproof) was not yet ruled out at time of writing.

40.title: MAJOR BREAKTHROUGH — Sub-chain A CONFIRMED: Peter McGivney Sr. (Derby CT) = son of Bryan McGivney (Creenow) + Reilly wife
40.summary: McGivney Family Newsletter (Oct 2000, via Find A Grave #188442550) states Peter McGivney Sr.'s father = 'Brian McGivney', mother = 'a Reilly'. Combined with Bryan McGivney's confirmed presence at Creenow (1828 Tithe Books, 1857 Griffith's) this CLOSES the Ireland-to-America gap: Bryan McGivney (Creenow) + Reilly wife -> Peter McGivney Sr. (b.1845 Co. Cavan, d.Sep 15 1898 Derby CT). Residual gap: Bryan-John brothers link and the Reilly wife's first name remain undocumented by direct record.

41.title: Ancestry.com CONFIRMS Patrick McGivney Sr.'s parents = John McGivney + Bridget Gibney — RESOLVES Peter/Patrick relationship as FIRST COUSINS (not brothers); Fr. McGivney + Peter George = SECOND COUSINS — FINAL
41.summary: RESOLVED (corrects Part 11/S90): Ancestry.com record shows Patrick McGivney Sr. (b.1832, d.Jun 6 1878 Waterbury CT) born Drumbee, Crosserlough Parish, to John McGivney + Bridget Gibney (not 'Bridget Sheridan' as WikiTree suggested). Peter McGivney Sr.'s confirmed father is Bryan McGivney (Creenow, S120). John and Bryan are brothers (strong circumstantial case — Crosserlough Parish, same family area; no direct document). Therefore: Patrick Sr. and Peter Sr. = FIRST COUSINS (withdrawing the earlier 'probable brothers' hypothesis); Fr. Michael J. McGivney (Patrick's son) and Peter George McGivney (Peter Sr.'s son) = SECOND COUSINS. Common ancestor: an unnamed McGivney patriarch, b.~1780-1790 County Cavan.

42.title: 1950 US Census confirms John J. Relihan + Anna Relihan household Bridgeport CT; Sub-chain C upgraded to "very strong"
42.summary: 1950 US Federal Census (MyHeritage) shows John J. Relihan head of household with wife Anna and children incl. Thomas Relihan (b.~1931) + 4 siblings in Bridgeport CT — matches all 5 known Dooley-branch children. Sub-chain C (John Relihan Sr. = Maurice Relihan's son) upgraded from 'highly probable' to 'very strong' circumstantial; still no document directly naming John as Maurice's son.

43.title: CT Death Index targeted query: JAMES RELIHAN d.Sep 10 1946 Derby CT = James T. Relihan (Maurice's son); MAURICE RELIHAN d.Aug 2 1921 Shelton CT confirmed
43.summary: CT Death Index confirms James T. Relihan d.Sep 10 1946 Derby CT and re-confirms Maurice Relihan d.Aug 2 1921 Shelton CT. Priority target for Sub-chain C documentary proof: James T.'s Sep 1946 Evening Sentinel obituary (Derby Public Library microfilm or GenealogyBank subscription; not in free LNV archive, which only covers through Jan 1931).

1-addendum-jul1.title: Jul 1 2026 (S66-S68) — un-numbered session immediately following Part 1: Donald J. Irwin confirmed, O'Connell branch (Joseph R. Relihan's parents) confirmed, PROBABLE BRANCH MERGER hypothesis first raised (Maurice Relihan's 1910 census household)
1-addendum-jul1.summary: Un-numbered content in the source (no 'Part N' label; sits between the Jun 30 session and the numbered Part 2). Business & Property tab added. Donald Jay Irwin confirmed (Yale Law 1954, CT Congressman 4th District 1959-61 & 1965-69, KofC member, b.Sep 7 1926, d.Jul 7 2013) as the probable 'Irwin' of 'Relihan & Irwin.' O'Connell branch confirmed: Joseph R. Relihan's parents = James T. Relihan + Mary O'Connell Relihan; O'Connell flagged as a McGivney-side bridge surname. PROBABLE BRANCH MERGER first raised: the 1910 Census (image from Betsy Relihan Gross) shows Maurice Relihan + Mary Dolan household, Derby CT, with 4 children: Mary A. (~1891), John (~1892), Helen (~1894, confirmed = Helen R. Relihan), James (~1895). James T. Relihan is almost certainly this 'James.' If also 'John' (~1892) = John R. Relihan Sr. (Dooley/Duley branch), all Relihan research branches descend from Maurice Relihan (b.1856 County Kerry) — this is the hypothesis that drives most of Parts 2-42's research.

2-pinned-pedigree.title: Pedigree Tables added to index.html (replacing tree.html D3 chart); McNamara + Kelly branches merged into the Dooley-branch tree per 'combine whenever common name' rule; O'Connell branch re-confirmed (Derby CT geography)
2-pinned-pedigree.summary: A second, distinct session is also labeled 'Part 2' in the source (dated Jul 2 2026, pinned near the top of the live Overview tab above Part 43 — a duplicate/inconsistent label from the site's own numbering, not a Night-3 error; recorded here under a disambiguating key rather than silently overwritten). Four pedigree tables added to index.html: (1) Grant's direct ancestor chain; (2) John Relihan Sr. — all children, Dooley/Duley + McNamara branches merged; (3) Maurice Relihan's children showing all CT branches at one level; (4) McGivney family. McNamara + Kelly branches were merged per the user's stated rule 'combine whenever common name': John J. Relihan Sr. (Fairfield CT, wife Mabel McNamara) was at this point treated as the SAME PERSON as John Relihan Sr. (Bridgeport CT, wife Ann Duley) under a since-DISPROVEN two-marriages hypothesis (disproven at Part 5 — see mcn-2/tpr-2 facts). O'Connell branch re-confirmed: James M. Relihan b.Aug 19 1927 Derby CT — strong geographic evidence for the O'Connell branch (James T. Relihan) as Maurice Relihan's son.

2-addendum-jul2.title: Jul 2 2026 (S75-S77) — un-numbered session: Rosanna McGivney Finn's 9 children fully documented; Fairfield Prep In Memoriam confirms 4 Relihan/Finn alumni; Rev. Vincent Finn's 1924-27 Derby CT posting confirmed
2-addendum-jul2.summary: Un-numbered content in the source (no 'Part N' label; sits between the numbered Part 3 and Part 2). Rosanna Frances McGivney Finn's 9 children fully documented via WikiTree McGivney-111 (S75): Joseph Patrick, John William, Edward James Jr., Msgr. Leo M. Finn (KofC Supreme Chaplain 1939-60), Anna C., Fr. Francis James Finn, Dr. Alfred Joseph Finn, Fr. Vincent Edward Finn, Zita M. Finn Burns — 3 of 9 were priests. Fairfield Prep In Memoriam confirms 4 Relihan/Finn alumni (S76): John R. Finn ('46) and Senator John J. Relihan Jr. ('46) were classmates; Joe R. Relihan ('47); a previously-unknown John J. Relihan ('58, later identified as the McNamara-branch AZ grandson). Rev. Vincent Finn (Rosanna's son, McGivney's grand-nephew) served as Assistant Pastor at St. Mary's Church, Derby CT 1924-1927 (S77) — the same parish where Helen Relihan married Peter George McGivney in June 1921, the closest social-proximity overlap yet found between the two families.
